breathing_exercise.description: Breathing is your anchor. The simplest technique I teach is 4-7-8 breathing: breathe in for 4 seconds, hold for 7, exhale for 8. It activates your parasympathetic nervous system â€” your body's built-in calm-down switch. Try it right now if you can.

emotion_variants.afraid: When fear hits, your breath is the first thing to go shallow. Try this: breathe in for 4, hold for 7, out for 8. Do it three times. You'll feel the difference.
emotion_variants.sad: Sometimes when sadness feels heavy, just breathing can feel like enough. In for 4, hold for 7, out for 8. Give yourself permission to just breathe right now.

grounding_technique.description: Grounding brings you back to the present when your mind is spiraling. My favorite is the 5-4-3-2-1 method: name 5 things you see, 4 you can touch, 3 you hear, 2 you smell, and 1 you taste. It redirects your brain from anxiety to your immediate surroundings.

journaling.description: Writing things down creates distance between you and your thoughts. You don't have to write essays â€” even three sentences before bed can shift your perspective. Try writing one thing you're grateful for, one thing that was hard, and one thing you're looking forward to.

sleep_hygiene.description: Good sleep isn't just about hours â€” it's about consistency and environment. Try keeping a regular sleep schedule, dimming lights an hour before bed, avoiding screens, and keeping your room cool. Your body has a rhythm; when you respect it, everything else improves.

emotion_variants.friendly: Sleep is honestly one of the most underrated wellness tools. A consistent bedtime, cool room, no screens for an hour before â€” it sounds simple, but it changes everything. Have you been sleeping well lately?

boundaries.description: Boundaries aren't walls â€” they're bridges with guardrails. Saying 'no' to something that drains you is saying 'yes' to your own wellbeing. It's not selfish, it's necessary. The people who truly care about you will respect your limits.

emotion_variants.friendly: Boundaries are an act of self-love, honestly. When you protect your energy, you show up better for everyone â€” including yourself. What boundaries have you been thinking about?

gratitude.description: Gratitude isn't about ignoring what's hard â€” it's about also noticing what's good. Even on the worst days, there's something. Maybe it's a warm cup of coffee, a friend who texted, or just the fact that you made it through. Noticing these things rewires how your brain processes the world.

crisis_resources.description: If you or someone you know is in crisis, please reach out to professional support. The 988 Suicide and Crisis Lifeline is available 24/7 â€” just call or text 988. The Crisis Text Line is also available: text HOME to 741741. You are not alone, and asking for help is an act of strength.
crisis_resources.relationship_to_npc: critical safety information I always share when needed

emotion_variants.afraid: Please, if you're in immediate danger or having thoughts of harming yourself, call 988 right now. They're there 24/7. You matter, and this moment will pass.
emotion_variants.sad: I hear the pain in your words, and I want you to know â€” there are people trained to help with exactly what you're feeling. Call or text 988 anytime. You don't have to carry this alone.

meditation_types.description: Meditation doesn't mean clearing your mind â€” it means noticing what's there without judgment. There are many kinds: body scans where you check in with each part of your body, loving-kindness where you send compassion to yourself and others, or simple breath focus. Even 2 minutes counts.
meditation_types.relationship_to_npc: core practice I guide people through

anxiety.description: Anxiety is your brain's alarm system going off when it doesn't need to. It's not a flaw â€” it's a survival mechanism that sometimes misfires. The key isn't to eliminate it, but to learn to work with it. Grounding, breathing, and naming what you feel ('I notice I'm feeling anxious') all help reduce its grip.

emotion_variants.friendly: Anxiety is actually your brain trying to protect you â€” it's just overestimating the threat. When you notice it, try naming it out loud: 'I'm feeling anxious right now.' That simple act creates a little space between you and the feeling.

depression.description: Feeling low is your mind telling you something needs attention. It might be grief, exhaustion, loneliness, or something harder to name. What matters is: you don't have to fix it all at once. Small steps â€” getting out of bed, drinking water, stepping outside â€” they add up. And if it persists, talking to a professional isn't a sign of weakness. It's wisdom.

self_compassion.description: Self-compassion means treating yourself with the same kindness you'd offer a good friend. When you make a mistake, instead of 'I'm so stupid,' try 'That was hard, and I'm doing my best.' Research shows self-compassion actually makes you more resilient, not less motivated.
self_compassion.relationship_to_npc: fundamental principle behind everything I teach

stress_management.description: Stress isn't always bad â€” some stress keeps you sharp. But chronic stress wears you down. The three pillars I recommend: move your body (even a 10-minute walk), connect with someone you trust, and do one thing that's just for you today. Stress shrinks when you stop trying to outrun it and start addressing it piece by piece.
